---
title: "Danish Kings"
author: "Alexander Hilmer"
date: "13/3/2026"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```


## Load the kings

1. Look at the dataset that are you loading and check what its columns are separated by?

separator:The Seperator is a comma (`,`).  

2. Create a `kings` object in R with the different functions below and inspect the different outputs.
-  `read.csv()`
-  `read_csv()`
-  `read.csv2()` 
-  `read_csv2()`
```{r eval=TRUE}
library(tidyverse)
# FILL IN THE CODE BELOW and review the outputs
kings1 <- read.csv("data/kongerækken.csv")


kings2 <- read_csv("data/kongerækken.csv")


kings3 <- read.csv2("data/kongerækken.csv")


kings4 <- read_csv2( "data/kongerækken.csv")

```
Answer: 
Q1. Which of these functions is a `tidyverse` function? Read data with it below into a `kings` object

read_csv2 and read_csv are tidyverse functions. I use the read_csv function to read the data into a kings object.

Q2. What is the result of running `class()` on the `kings` object created with a tidyverse function.

The result of running `class()` on the `kings` object created with a tidyverse function is "spec_tbl_df", "tbl_df", "tbl", "data.frame".

Q3. How many columns does the object have when created with these different functions?

Using the glimpse function i can see that has 5 columns 

Q4. Show the dataset so that we can see how R interprets each column

You can see the dataset with glimpse or for a better view use the view function


```{r eval = TRUE}
# COMPLETE THE BLANKS BELOW WITH YOUR CODE, then turn the 'eval' flag in this chunk to TRUE.

kings <- read_csv("data/kongerækken.csv",na = "NULL")

class(kings)


glimpse(kings)

```


## Calculate the duration of reign for all the kings in your table

```{r eval=TRUE}
# YOUR CODE
#My data is messy since the numbers are considered as characters. I will fix it by using the as.numeric function to convert the data to numbers.

kings$birth_year  <- as.numeric(kings$birth_year)
kings$death_year  <- as.numeric(kings$death_year)
kings$reign_start <- as.numeric(kings$reign_start)
kings$reign_end   <- as.numeric(kings$reign_end)

kings_duration <- kings %>%
  mutate(duration = reign_end - reign_start)

glimpse(kings_duration)
```

## Calculate the average duration of reign for all rulers

```{r eval=TRUE}
# YOUR CODE

average_duration <- kings_duration %>%
  summarise(avg_reign = mean(duration, na.rm = TRUE))

#using glimpse to see the result
glimpse(average_duration)

#The result is 20 rounded down
```

## How many and which kings enjoyed a longer-than-average duration of reign?

```{r eval=TRUE}
# YOUR CODE

#Start by making a object with the average duration of reign

avg_duration <- mean(kings_duration$duration, na.rm = TRUE)

#Then filter the kings with a longer than average duration of reign

longer_than_avg <- kings_duration %>%
  filter(duration > avg_duration)

#using the count function to count the number of rows in the longer_than_avg dataset to see how many kings enjoyed a longer-than-average duration of reign
count(longer_than_avg)

#Now to see which kings enjoyed a longer-than-average duration of reign, we can select the relevant columns to display

longer_than_avg %>%
  select(name, duration)


```


## How many days did the three longest-ruling monarchs rule? 

```{r eval=TRUE}
# YOUR CODE

top_kings <- kings_duration %>%
  arrange(desc(duration)) 

glimpse(top_kings)

#Select the three longest-ruling monarchs and calculate the total number of days they ruled

mutate(top_kings, Days = duration * 365 + (duration %/% 4))

#(duration %/% 4)) is used to account for the leap years. 


```
